## Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Exemptions

Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Missouri's exemptions is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions - you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Missouri State Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $15,000

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $30,000. This is one of the lowest homestead exemptions among major states.

RSMo 513.475

Motor Vehicle - $3,000

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Missouri Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings$3,000 total

Clothing$1,500

Tools of trade$3,000

Jewelry$1,500 (wedding rings)

Wildcard$600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Special Notes

Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions - federal exemptions are not available. Missouri's $15,000 homestead exemption is among the lowest in the country.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Missouri homestead exemption?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $30,000. This is one of the lowest homestead exemptions among major states.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Missouri?

Missouri's motor vehicle exemption is $3,000. If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Missouri allow federal exemptions?

No. Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions only.

## Missouri Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/faq.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Faq

Missouri Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Missouri residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Missouri?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri protects $3,000 in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri has 2 districts: W.D. Mo., E.D. Mo.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Missouri median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Find Filings

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Missouri

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Missouri - from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the Eastern and Western Districts of Missouri clerk's office. The Missouri bankruptcy court website is https://www.mow.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Missouri court: https://ecf.mow.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Missouri bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the Eastern and Western Districts of Missouri bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.mow.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Missouri?

Bankruptcy filings in Missouri are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The Eastern and Western Districts of Missouri has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.mow.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Missouri?

Bankruptcy cases in Missouri are filed in the Eastern and Western Districts of Missouri. The court website is https://www.mow.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.mow.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

## Missouri Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Missouri Bankruptcy Guide›Glossary

Missouri Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Missouri filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in W.D. Mo. and E.D. Mo..

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Missouri homestead exemption: $15,000; vehicle: $3,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Missouri median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Check Missouri exemption statutes for availability.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable - student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Missouri

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Missouri Bankruptcy Guide›Income Limits

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Missouri

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Missouri and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Missouri median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Missouri median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 - No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income - wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Missouri?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Missouri median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling - anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Missouri median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level - higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

Means Test Guide
Discharge Screener

## Missouri Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

Missouri Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Missouri ranks above the national average for bankruptcy filings per capita, with particularly high filing rates in the St. Louis and Kansas City metro areas. This free guide covers everything Missouri residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

2

Federal Districts

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Missouri Federal Court Districts

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. Where you live determines where you file.

Western District of Missouri (W.D. Mo.)

over 80,000 cases

Courthouse: 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Phone: (816) 512-1800 | mow.uscourts.gov

Covers Kansas City and western Missouri. Chapter 13 dismissal rate is 40.4% (2008-2019 mature data).

Eastern District of Missouri (E.D. Mo.)

over 120,000 cases

Courthouse: 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

Phone: (314) 244-4500 | moeb.uscourts.gov

Covers St. Louis and eastern Missouri.

Missouri Exemptions at a Glance

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. For married couples filing jointly, the exemption doubles to $30,000. This is one of the lowest homestead exemptions among major states.

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Wildcard$600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Missouri?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13 in Missouri

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Missouri?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Missouri typically range from $1,000 to $2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Missouri?

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts: W.D. Mo., E.D. Mo.. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Missouri Bankruptcy Guide›Judgments

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Missouri

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Missouri.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Missouri

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the $15,000 homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $3,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the W.D. Mo. and E.D. Mo. court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Missouri. The underlying debt determines dischargeability - if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Missouri, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: $15,000) and vehicles (exemption: $3,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener

## The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Kansas City - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/341-meeting.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Kansas City

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the trustee asks questions under oath. Most last 5-10 minutes.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting (named after 11 U.S.C. section 341) is a required hearing in every bankruptcy case. The trustee assigned to your case asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, income, and the information in your petition.

Despite its formal name, the 341 meeting is not held in front of a judge. Most meetings in Kansas City last only 5-10 minutes.

Kansas City 341 Meeting Location

W.D. Mo. - Kansas City Division: 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Many 341 meetings are now conducted by phone or video conference. Your attorney will confirm the format.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID (driver's license or passport)

Proof of Social Security number (SS card, W-2, or 1099)

Most recent tax return

Recent pay stubs (last 60 days)

Bank statements

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks questions under oath about your finances and assets.

Where is the 341 meeting held in Kansas City?

341 meetings for the W.D. Mo. Kansas City division are held at 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106, or by phone/video.

What should I bring?

Government-issued photo ID, proof of Social Security number, and copies of your most recent tax return and pay stubs.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## The Automatic Stay in Kansas City Bankruptcy - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/automatic-stay.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Kansas City Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay goes into effect. It immediately stops most creditor actions against you.

What the Automatic Stay Stops

Stopped by the Stay

Wage garnishment

Foreclosure

Vehicle repossession

Lawsuits

Creditor calls and letters

Bank account levies

Utility shutoffs (20 days)

IRS collection

NOT Stopped

Child support collection

Criminal proceedings

Tax audits (but no collection)

Pension loan repayment

Post-judgment evictions

Stay Violations

Creditors who violate the stay can face sanctions under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k), including actual damages, attorney fees, and punitive damages. Document everything and notify your attorney immediately.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay?

A federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect instantly when you file and stops most collection actions.

What does the automatic stay stop?

Wage garnishment, foreclosure, repossession, lawsuits, creditor calls, bank levies, utility shutoffs (20 days), and IRS collection.

What does the stay NOT stop?

Child support and alimony collection, criminal proceedings, certain tax audits, pension loan repayment, and post-judgment evictions.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Payment Plans, Dismissal Rates - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/chapter-13.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Kansas City

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. But in the Western District of Missouri, the dismissal rate is alarmingly high.

40.4% of W.D. Mo. Chapter 13 cases are dismissed

40.4%

WDMO Dismissal Rate (2008-2019)

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

$2.5-4K

Typical Attorney Fees

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to creditors.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Chapter 13 dismissal rate in Kansas City?

The Western District of Missouri has a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate based on mature 2008-2019 data. A dismissed case means the debtor got no debt relief despite months or years of payments.

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Plans last 3-5 years. If below state median income, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above, your plan must be 5 years.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. You can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

The automatic stay ends, creditors resume collection, and you receive no discharge. You may be able to file again but there may be waiting periods.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/chapter-7.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Kansas City

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Kansas City residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

In Kansas City, the vast majority of Chapter 7 filers keep all their property because it falls within Missouri exemption limits.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Missouri state median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation determines eligibility

For a single filer in Missouri, the current median is approximately $56,847/year. These figures increase with household size.

Full means test guide for Kansas City →

Missouri Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep. Missouri uses its own exemption system.

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000 equity

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Clothing$1,500

Wildcard$600 + up to $1,250 unused homestead

Tools of trade$3,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Public benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. Most last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - Court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, or debts from fraud. See section 523(a).

What is the means test for Chapter 7?

The means test compares your income to the Missouri median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed calculation determines eligibility. Full means test guide.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Kansas City?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing, and discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7?

Missouri exemptions protect: homestead equity up to $15,000, vehicle up to $3,000, household goods up to $3,000, wildcard of $600 plus unused homestead, and retirement accounts. Full details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13

## Kansas City Bankruptcy Filing Checklist - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/checklist.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Checklist

Kansas City Bankruptcy Filing Checklist

A step-by-step checklist for Kansas City residents preparing to file bankruptcy. Covers documents, credit counseling, and filing requirements.

Documents You Will Need

Tax returns - last 2 years

Pay stubs - last 6 months

Bank statements - last 6 months

Mortgage statements or lease agreement

Vehicle titles and loan statements

Credit card and loan statements

Medical bills

Court judgments and garnishment orders

Insurance policies

Retirement account statements

Steps Before Filing

Complete credit counseling - from an approved agency ($15-25). Required within 180 days before filing.

Gather all documents - see list above.

List all debts - every creditor, amount, and account number.

List all assets - everything you own and its approximate value.

Review exemptions - determine what is protected under Missouri exemptions.

Choose Chapter 7 or 13 - based on income, assets, and goals.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Do not transfer property before filing - this can be reversed and may constitute fraud

Do not pay back family members - preferential transfers can be clawed back by the trustee

Do not run up new debt - charges made shortly before filing may not be dischargeable

List all debts and assets - failure to disclose is perjury and can result in denial of discharge

Complete both courses - credit counseling before filing and debtor education before discharge

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need for bankruptcy?

Tax returns (last 2 years), pay stubs (last 6 months), bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, credit card statements, medical bills, and a list of all debts and assets.

Do I need credit counseling before filing?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved agency within 180 days before filing. Cost is typically $15-25.

What are common mistakes?

Transferring property before filing, paying back family members, running up new debt, failing to list all debts and assets, and not completing required courses.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting

## Bankruptcy Cost in Kansas City - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/cost.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kansas City?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

$1K-$4K

Typical Attorney Fees

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000 - $2,000Typical Kansas City range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Total estimate$1,400 - $2,400

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500 - $4,000Mostly paid through plan

Credit counseling$25 - $50Two courses required

Total estimate$2,900 - $4,400Most paid through plan, not upfront

Legal Aid Resources

Legal Aid of Western Missouri - free bankruptcy representation for qualifying low-income individuals

Kansas City Metropolitan Bar Association - lawyer referral service

## Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions - Kansas City
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/exemptions.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Exemptions

Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Missouri uses its own exemption system - you cannot choose federal exemptions.

Missouri Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - $15,000

Protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. Married couples filing jointly get $30,000.

Example: Home worth $200,000, mortgage owed $190,000. Equity = $10,000 - fully protected.

RSMo 513.475

Motor Vehicle - $3,000

Protects up to $3,000 of equity in one motor vehicle.

RSMo 513.430(1)

Wildcard - $600 + Up to $1,250

Protect an additional $600 of any property, plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead. Maximum: $1,850.

RSMo 513.430(3)

Other Missouri Exemptions

PropertyAmountStatute

Household goods, furnishings$3,000 totalRSMo 513.430(1)

Clothing$1,500RSMo 513.430(1)

Tools of trade$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

Wedding rings$1,500RSMo 513.430(1)

Health aidsUnlimitedRSMo 513.430(8)

Retirement accountsUnlimitedRSMo 513.430(10)(e)

Social SecurityUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Unemployment benefitsUnlimitedRSMo 288.380

Workers' compensationUnlimitedRSMo 287.260

Life insurance proceedsUnlimited (spouse/child beneficiary)RSMo 513.430(7)

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Kansas City Bankruptcy FAQ - Common Questions Answered - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/faq.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›FAQ

Kansas City Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions from Kansas City residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Kansas City?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to the Missouri median. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically. Means test guide.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After Chapter 7: wait 8 years for another 7 or 4 years for 13. After Chapter 13: wait 6 years for 7 or 2 years for another 13. Check eligibility.

Property and Assets

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity. In Chapter 13, you keep your home and catch up on payments. See exemptions.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

Missouri protects $3,000 in vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you keep your car. Keep your car guide.

What happens to my tax refund?

In Chapter 7, your refund may be an asset claimed by the trustee if it exceeds exemptions. In Chapter 13, it may need to be turned over. Timing your filing matters.

The Process

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years. The 341 meeting happens about 30 days after filing.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay takes effect immediately when you file. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops wage garnishments immediately upon filing. Learn more.

Types of Debt

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt guide.

Will bankruptcy eliminate student loans?

Generally no, except in cases of undue hardship. Student loan guide.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit?

Chapter 7 stays on your report for 10 years; Chapter 13 for 7. Many filers see credit improve within 1-2 years.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test
Cost Guide
Dismissed?
File Again?
How to File

Free, open-source bankruptcy transparency. No ads. No affiliate links. Supported by donations.

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024).

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Bankruptcy in Kansas City, Missouri - Complete Guide
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Bankruptcy in Kansas City, Missouri

Kansas City residents file in the Western District of Missouri. With a 40.4% Chapter 13 dismissal rate and unique state-line dynamics, understanding your options is critical. This free guide covers everything KC residents need to know.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

91,497

KC Area Cases (2008-2024)

40.4%

MO Side Dismissal Rate

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Kansas City Courthouse

Western District of Missouri - Kansas City Division

Courthouse: 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Phone: (816) 512-1800 | mow.uscourts.gov

All bankruptcy filings for Kansas City residents on the Missouri side are handled here. Kansas-side residents file in the District of Kansas (500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101).

Missouri Exemptions at a Glance

Missouri uses its own exemption system - you cannot choose federal exemptions. Key exemptions for Kansas City filers:

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Wildcard$600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Kansas City?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Kansas City?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments. Learn more.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Kansas City?

Missouri-side residents file in the Western District of Missouri (400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106). Kansas-side residents file in the District of Kansas (500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101).

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

See all frequently asked questions →

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Can I Keep My Car in Kansas City Bankruptcy? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/keep-your-car.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Keep Your Car

Can I Keep My Car in Kansas City Bankruptcy?

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. Missouri protects $3,000 of vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you keep your car regardless of equity.

Short answer: In most cases, yes, you can keep your car in bankruptcy.

Missouri Vehicle Exemption: $3,000

Missouri protects up to $3,000 of equity in one motor vehicle (RSMo 513.430). Equity = fair market value minus amount owed. If you owe more than the car is worth, your equity is $0 - fully protected.

The wildcard exemption ($600 plus unused homestead up to $1,250) can add up to $1,850 more in protection.

Options in Chapter 7

Reaffirmation

Sign a new agreement to keep the car and continue payments. The debt survives your discharge.

+ Keep your car

- Debt survives bankruptcy

Redemption

Pay the car's fair market value in a lump sum under 11 U.S.C. section 722. Keep the car free and clear.

+ Save thousands on underwater loans

- Requires lump-sum cash

Surrender

Return the car. Deficiency balance is discharged.

+ Clean break

- You lose the vehicle

Chapter 13 Cramdown

If you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing Chapter 13, you can reduce the loan to the car's current fair market value. You pay only what the car is worth through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy?

In most cases, yes. Missouri protects $3,000 of vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, you keep your car and catch up on payments.

What is the Missouri vehicle exemption?

Missouri allows $3,000 per person under RSMo 513.430. You can add the wildcard for additional protection.

What is a cramdown?

In Chapter 13, if you bought your car more than 910 days before filing, you can reduce the loan to fair market value.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Can I Keep My House in Kansas City Bankruptcy? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/keep-your-house.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Keep Your House

Can I Keep My House in Kansas City Bankruptcy?

Missouri protects up to $15,000 of home equity. In Chapter 13, you can save your home from foreclosure by catching up on missed payments.

In many cases, yes. Chapter 13 is specifically designed to help homeowners save their homes.

Missouri Homestead Exemption: $15,000

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence (RSMo 513.475). Married couples filing jointly: $30,000. Equity = home value minus mortgage owed.

Example: Home worth $200,000, mortgage $190,000. Equity = $10,000 - fully protected.

Saving Your Home with Chapter 13

Chapter 13 is often called the "save your home" chapter:

The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately

Catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years

Continue making current payments going forward

Lien stripping may remove a second mortgage if your home is underwater

Missouri allows non-judicial foreclosure, which can be completed in 60-90 days. Filing before a scheduled sale is critical.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house in bankruptcy?

If your equity is within the $15,000 Missouri exemption, yes in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your home regardless and catch up on payments.

What is the Missouri homestead exemption?

Missouri protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence. Married couples get $30,000.

How does Chapter 13 save my home?

The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately. Your repayment plan includes catching up on missed payments over 3-5 years.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Kansas City Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/means-test.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Means Test

Kansas City Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy. It compares your income to the Missouri state median for your household size.

2026 Missouri Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual MedianMonthly

1 person$56,847$4,737

2 persons$72,036$6,003

3 persons$85,023$7,085

4 persons$104,670$8,723

Each additionalAdd ~$9,900

Missouri filers: W.D. Mo. Courthouse, 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106.

Step-by-Step Walkthrough

Calculate your current monthly income (CMI) - Add up all income from the past 6 full calendar months. Divide by 6 for monthly, multiply by 12 for annual.

Determine household size - Count everyone who depends on your income for support.

Compare to median - If at or below the median, you pass and qualify for Chapter 7.

If above: Calculate deductions - Subtract IRS-approved expenses, secured debt payments, and priority debts.

Calculate disposable income - Multiply remaining monthly disposable income by 60. If less than $9,075, you pass.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the means test?

The means test compares your income to Missouri median income. If below, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

What is the Missouri median income for 2026?

Single filer: $56,847. 2-person: $72,036. 3-person: $85,023. 4-person: $104,670.

What if I fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13 instead, wait until income drops, or challenge with special circumstances.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

Ch. 7 vs 13

341 Meeting

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/medical-debt.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Medical Debt

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Kansas City

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. It is one of the most common reasons Kansas City residents file.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely at discharge (3-4 months). In Chapter 13, you may pay a portion through your plan with the rest discharged at completion.

The automatic stay stops all medical debt collection activity immediately upon filing, including phone calls, letters, lawsuits, and wage garnishment.

Kansas City Hospital Charity Care

Before filing bankruptcy, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance:

Truman Medical Centers - charity care for patients below 200% of federal poverty level

Saint Luke's Health System - financial assistance program

Research Medical Center - community care program

The University of Kansas Health System - financial assistance for qualifying patients

Frequently Asked Questions

Can medical debt be discharged in bankruptcy?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Is medical debt the top reason for bankruptcy?

Medical debt is consistently cited as a leading cause, with 62-66% of bankruptcies connected to medical expenses.

Are there charity care programs at KC hospitals?

Yes. Truman Medical Centers, Saint Luke's, and Research Medical Center all offer charity care for qualifying patients.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Kansas City - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/student-loans.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Student Loans

Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Kansas City

Student loans are generally not dischargeable in bankruptcy except through an adversary proceeding proving undue hardship.

The Undue Hardship Standard

To discharge student loans, you must file a separate adversary proceeding and prove "undue hardship." Most courts (including the Western District of Missouri) have historically used the Brunner test:

You cannot maintain a minimal standard of living if forced to repay

Additional circumstances exist indicating this situation will persist

You have made good faith efforts to repay the loans

Recent DOJ guidance (2022-2023) has made it somewhat easier to get government agreement to discharge student loans, but it remains challenging.

Alternatives to Bankruptcy for Student Loans

Income-Driven Repayment (IDR) - payments capped at a percentage of discretionary income

SAVE Plan - newest IDR plan with lower payments for many borrowers

Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF) - forgiveness after 120 qualifying payments while working for government or nonprofit

Borrower Defense to Repayment - discharge if your school misled you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can student loans be discharged?

Generally no, except through an adversary proceeding proving undue hardship. Recent legal developments have made it somewhat easier in some courts.

What is the undue hardship standard?

Most courts use the Brunner test requiring (1) inability to maintain minimal standard of living, (2) persistent circumstances, and (3) good faith repayment efforts.

What alternatives exist?

Income-driven repayment plans, Public Service Loan Forgiveness, borrower defense, and the SAVE plan may reduce or eliminate student loan obligations without bankruptcy.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

OBP Home
1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13

## Bankruptcy in Blue Springs, MO - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/suburbs/blue-springs.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Blue Springs

Bankruptcy in Blue Springs, MO

Blue Springs residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the Kansas City metro area, Blue Springs filers are served by the Western District of Missouri.

57,543

Population (est.)

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$3,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

Western District of Missouri

400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Missouri Exemptions

PropertyExemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + unused homestead

RetirementFully exempt

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $1,000-$2,000.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Blue Springs residents file for bankruptcy?

The Western District of Missouri at 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

How much does bankruptcy cost in Blue Springs?

The same as anywhere in Missouri. Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Missouri state exemptions. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Full list.

## Bankruptcy in Leawood, KS - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/suburbs/leawood.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Leawood

Bankruptcy in Leawood, KS

Leawood residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the Kansas City metro area, Leawood filers are served by the District of Kansas.

34,659

Population (est.)

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

$20,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

District of Kansas

500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

Kansas Jurisdiction Note

Leawood is in Kansas, not Missouri. Leawood residents file in the District of Kansas (500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101), not the Western District of Missouri. Kansas has different exemptions - notably an unlimited homestead exemption and a $20,000 vehicle exemption. We include Leawood in the Kansas City metro guide because it is part of the greater KC area.

Kansas Exemptions

PropertyExemption

HomesteadUnlimited (1 acre urban)

Vehicle$20,000 / $40,000 married

Household goodsNo per-item limit

RetirementFully exempt

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $1,000-$2,000.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Leawood residents file for bankruptcy?

The District of Kansas at 500 State Ave, Kansas City, KS 66101

How much does bankruptcy cost in Leawood?

The same as anywhere in Kansas. Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Kansas state exemptions. Homestead: unlimited. Vehicle: $20,000. Full list.

Kansas state exemptions. Homestead: unlimited. Vehicle: $20,000. Full list.

Check Your Eligibility

## Kansas City Bankruptcy Timeline - What to Expect - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/timeline.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Timeline

Kansas City Bankruptcy Timeline - What to Expect

A detailed timeline of the bankruptcy process in Kansas City. From preparation through discharge, here is what to expect at each stage.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-4: Consultation and Preparation - Meet with an attorney. Gather documents. Complete credit counseling ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect immediately. All collection stops.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors at 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City. Usually 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor Education - Complete the second required financial course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - Court enters discharge order. Case closed shortly after.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Credit counseling, document gathering, plan preparation.

Day 1: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed.

Day 30: First plan payment to trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments. Report income changes.

Plan completion: Debtor education course. Discharge entered.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting occurs about 30 days after filing.

How long does Chapter 13 take?

Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. Plan confirmation usually happens 45-120 days after filing.

When does the automatic stay take effect?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the court - not when creditors find out.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Stop Wage Garnishment in Kansas City - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/kansas-city/wage-garnishment.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Kansas City›Wage Garnishment

Stop Wage Garnishment in Kansas City

The automatic stay stops wage garnishment the instant you file bankruptcy. Your employer must stop withholding garnished wages.

The Automatic Stay Stops Garnishment Immediately

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, the moment you file bankruptcy, all wage garnishment for pre-bankruptcy debts must stop. Your attorney notifies your employer and the garnishing creditor. This applies to both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Exception: Garnishments for child support, alimony, and certain tax debts may continue.

Missouri Garnishment Limits

In Missouri, creditors can garnish the lesser of:

25% of your disposable earnings, or

The amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30x the federal minimum wage ($7.25/hr = $217.50/week)

Head of household exemptions may provide additional protection. Filing bankruptcy eliminates the garnishment entirely for qualifying debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

How does bankruptcy stop garnishment?

The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 immediately prohibits all wage garnishment for pre-bankruptcy debts.

How much can be garnished in Missouri?

The lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount exceeding 30 times federal minimum wage ($217.50/week).

How quickly does it stop?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant the petition is filed. Most employers stop within 1-2 pay periods of receiving notice.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Missouri - Discharge, Alternatives, Protections
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Missouri

Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Missouri

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. It is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. You do not need to feel guilty about discharging medical debt - the system is designed for exactly this situation.

Quick answer: Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. You cannot file only for medical debt (all eligible debts are included), but medical bills are unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. Before filing, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance programs in Missouri.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy: The Basics

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured, non-priority debt in bankruptcy - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This is the most favorable classification for discharge:

Chapter 7: Medical debt is completely eliminated when you receive your discharge (typically 3-4 months after filing)

Chapter 13: Medical debt is paid at whatever percentage your plan pays to unsecured creditors. In many Chapter 13 plans, unsecured creditors receive 10% or less. A $50,000 medical bill might result in paying $5,000 or less over 3-5 years.

No exception to discharge: Unlike student loans or certain tax debts, there is no exception that prevents medical debt from being discharged

Missouri Medical Debt Protections

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in Missouri bankruptcy. Missouri does not have specific medical debt protection statutes comparable to some states. Missouri hospitals receiving Hill-Burton funds must provide charity care. Missouri expanded Medicaid in 2021 under the ACA (MO HealthNet), which has helped reduce medical debt. The Missouri Hospital Association has voluntary charity care guidelines. Medical debt remains a leading driver of Missouri bankruptcy filings.

Before Filing: Exhaust Other Options

Hospital Financial Assistance (Charity Care)

Under the ACA, nonprofit hospitals are required to have financial assistance policies. Many hospitals will significantly reduce or eliminate bills for patients below certain income thresholds (typically 200-400% of the federal poverty level). Ask the billing department about financial assistance before paying or filing bankruptcy.

Negotiate the Bill

Medical bills are often negotiable. Strategies include:

Request an itemized bill and dispute any errors

Ask for the "self-pay" or "uninsured" rate (often 40-60% less than the billed amount)

Offer a lump-sum settlement for less than the full amount

Request a payment plan with no interest

When Bankruptcy Makes More Sense

Bankruptcy is often the better choice when:

Medical debt is combined with other unsecured debts (credit cards, personal loans)

Total debt exceeds your ability to repay within a reasonable time

Creditors are suing or garnishing wages

The hospital denies charity care or the remaining balance is still unaffordable

Medical debt is on top of existing financial stress

Credit Report Changes for Medical Debt

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Medical debt under $500: No longer reported by Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion (as of 2023)

Paid medical collections: Removed from credit reports

1-year waiting period: Medical debt cannot appear on credit reports until at least 1 year after going to collections

FICO 10T and VantageScore 4.0: Newer scoring models reduce the weight of medical collections

Despite these improvements, large medical debts sent to third-party collectors can still damage your credit. Bankruptcy provides a definitive resolution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical debt?

You cannot selectively discharge only medical debt - all eligible unsecured debts are included. But if medical debt is your primary problem, bankruptcy eliminates it along with other unsecured debts.

Is medical debt dischargeable?

Yes, 100%. Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. There is no exception preventing discharge.

Should I negotiate or file bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only debt, try negotiation and charity care first. If you have multiple debts or the amounts are unmanageable, bankruptcy may be more efficient.

Will medical debt affect my credit?

Medical debt under $500 is no longer reported. Paid medical collections are removed. Large unpaid amounts can still affect credit until resolved through payment, settlement, or bankruptcy.

Does Missouri have medical debt protections?

Protections vary. Check with your hospital about financial assistance programs. Nonprofit hospitals are required to have charity care policies under federal law.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Missouri Bankruptcy Guide›Payday Loans

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Missouri

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy - meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Missouri. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener
Missouri Chapter 7 Guide
