## Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Missouri - 60-Day Rescission, Options
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Missouri

Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Missouri

Reaffirmation agreements make you personally liable for a debt that would otherwise be discharged. If you regret signing one, you may have options - especially if you are still within the 60-day rescission window.

Quick answer: You can rescind (cancel) a reaffirmation agreement within 60 days of filing it with the court, or before discharge, whichever is later (11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4)). No reason needed - just send written notice to the creditor. After 60 days, options are limited but include negotiation, voluntary surrender, or a new filing.

The 60-Day Rescission Window

Under 11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4), you have an absolute right to rescind any reaffirmation agreement at any time before discharge or within 60 days after the agreement is filed with the court, whichever is later.

No reason required - you do not have to explain why

Written notice to the creditor is sufficient

The debt becomes dischargeable again once rescinded

The creditor may repossess the collateral (vehicle, etc.) after rescission

If you are considering rescission, act quickly. Count the days carefully from when the agreement was filed with the court (check your case docket), not when you signed it.

Options After the 60-Day Window

If You Cannot Afford the Payments

After the rescission window closes, you have several options:

Negotiate with the creditor: Request lower payments, reduced interest, or loan modification. Creditors often prefer this to repossession/foreclosure.

Refinance: If your credit has improved since filing, refinancing at a lower rate may make payments affordable.

Voluntary surrender: Return the property. You will owe any deficiency balance (the difference between what you owe and what the property sells for). This is a regular debt - not discharged because you reaffirmed it.

File a new bankruptcy: If eligible under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) (Chapter 7: 8 years between filings) or section 1328(f) (Chapter 13: varies), the reaffirmed debt can be discharged in the new case.

Missouri-Specific Considerations

Missouri uses state exemptions only. Missouri has a generous motor vehicle exemption of $3,000 (RSMo 513.430(1)(5)). The W.D. Mo. and E.D. Mo. courts both review reaffirmation agreements. The 60-day rescission period applies. Missouri Merchandising Practices Act (RSMo 407) provides additional consumer remedies against deceptive practices during the reaffirmation process.

Common Reaffirmation Regrets

Vehicle Reaffirmation

The most common reaffirmation regret involves vehicles. Debtors reaffirm a car loan to keep the vehicle but later realize the payment is unsustainable or the car is worth far less than the loan balance. Without reaffirmation, the debt would have been discharged - you could have surrendered the car and owed nothing.

Mortgage Reaffirmation

Most attorneys advise against mortgage reaffirmation. Without reaffirmation, you can keep making payments and stay in the home. If you ever need to walk away, the deficiency is discharged. With reaffirmation, you are personally liable for any deficiency after foreclosure.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I undo a reaffirmation agreement?

Within 60 days of filing or before discharge: yes, automatically. After that: options are limited to negotiation, refinancing, surrender, or a new bankruptcy filing.

What happens after the 60-day window?

The agreement is binding. You can negotiate with the creditor, refinance, voluntarily surrender, or file a new bankruptcy if eligible.

Do I need a reason to rescind?

No. The right to rescind within 60 days is absolute - no reason or justification needed. Simply send written notice to the creditor.

What if the court approved my reaffirmation?

Court approval does not prevent rescission during the 60-day window. The right to rescind under section 524(c)(4) applies regardless of court approval.

Should I have reaffirmed in the first place?

That depends on the specific asset and your financial situation. Many attorneys advise against reaffirmation (especially on mortgages) because it restores personal liability on debt that could have been discharged.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Renting After Bankruptcy in Missouri - Landlord Screening, Tips, Tenant Rights
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Renting After Bankruptcy in Missouri

Renting After Bankruptcy in Missouri

Bankruptcy does not prevent you from renting. Many landlords will approve applicants with a bankruptcy on their record if you can show stable income and a reliable rental history. Here is how to navigate the process in Missouri.

Quick answer: You can rent after bankruptcy. Landlords can see the filing on your credit report, but it is not an automatic disqualifier. Focus on stable income, rental references, an honest explanation, and (if needed) a larger security deposit. Government housing assistance cannot be denied based on bankruptcy under section 525(a).

What Landlords See on Your Credit Report

When a landlord runs a credit check, they will see:

The bankruptcy filing - Chapter 7 (10 years) or Chapter 13 (7 years)

Discharged debts - accounts included in the bankruptcy will show as "included in bankruptcy" or "discharged"

Your credit score - typically drops significantly at filing but recovers over time

Post-filing payment history - new accounts opened after filing show your current reliability

Many landlords focus more on recent history than old entries. A bankruptcy from 2-3 years ago with perfect payment history since then is viewed very differently than a recent filing.

Missouri Tenant Rights and Protections

Missouri has minimal tenant protection laws. Missouri does not have a comprehensive landlord-tenant act like some states. The Missouri Landlord-Tenant Law (RSMo Ch. 441) provides basic protections. There is no rent control in Missouri. Landlords can check credit reports and consider bankruptcy history. Kansas City and St. Louis metro areas have competitive rental markets. Providing strong rental references and proof of income helps offset a bankruptcy on your record.

Tips for Getting Approved

1. Prepare a Rental Resume

Include your rental history, employment details, income verification, and references from previous landlords. A well-organized application signals reliability.

2. Write a Brief Explanation Letter

Address the bankruptcy directly. Explain the circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and emphasize what has changed. Show that you have stable income and have been managing finances responsibly since filing.

3. Offer a Larger Security Deposit

Offering an additional month's deposit reduces the landlord's risk and shows good faith. Check Missouri law for any limits on security deposit amounts.

4. Consider Individual Landlords Over Large Complexes

Individual landlords and small property owners often have more flexibility in their screening criteria than large corporate-managed apartment complexes that use automated approval algorithms.

5. Get References

A letter from a previous landlord confirming on-time payments and good tenancy can outweigh a credit blemish. Employment references showing stable income also help.

Government Housing After Bankruptcy

Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based solely on a bankruptcy filing. This includes:

Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers

Public housing

State housing assistance programs

FHA-insured mortgages (after applicable waiting periods)

The waiting period for FHA loans is 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I rent after bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes. Many landlords will approve applicants with bankruptcy if you show stable income, good rental history, and an honest explanation of your filing.

How long does bankruptcy show on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. The impact on your score diminishes over time, especially with good post-filing payment history.

Can a landlord deny me for bankruptcy?

For new applications, landlords can consider bankruptcy as part of screening in most states. For renewals, some courts extend section 525 protections. Government housing cannot be denied based on bankruptcy.

Should I explain the bankruptcy to a landlord?

Yes. A brief, honest explanation shows responsibility and maturity. Emphasize what changed and your current financial stability.

Can I get Section 8 after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based on bankruptcy. Section 8, public housing, and other government housing programs remain available.

Related Topics

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Repo & Credit Report

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Missouri

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report - but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged - you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Missouri credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Missouri?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Missouri?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Missouri.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

Related Topics

## Missouri Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/resources.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Resources

Missouri Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Missouri.

Bankruptcy Courts

Western District of Missouri

Address: 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106

Phone: (816) 512-1800

Website: mow.uscourts.gov

Eastern District of Missouri

Address: 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102

Phone: (314) 244-4500

Website: moeb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Legal Aid of Western Missouri

Free legal services for the Kansas City metro area.

Phone: (816) 474-6750

Website: lawmo.org

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri

Free legal services for the St. Louis metro area.

Phone: (314) 534-4200

Website: lsem.org

Legal Services of Southern Missouri

Free legal services for southern Missouri.

Phone: (800) 444-4863

Website: lsosm.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 13: (816) 512-1940

Website: justice.gov/ust

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Missouri?

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy courts. W.D. Mo.: 400 E. 9th St, Kansas City, MO 64106 ((816) 512-1800); E.D. Mo.: 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis, MO 63102 ((314) 244-4500)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes. Legal Aid of Western Missouri ((816) 474-6750) provides free legal services. Legal Services of Eastern Missouri ((314) 534-4200) provides free legal services. Legal Services of Southern Missouri ((800) 444-4863) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Missouri Bankruptcy?
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Missouri

Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Missouri

Your retirement savings are among the most protected assets in bankruptcy. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law, and IRAs are protected up to $1,512,350. Here is what Missouri filers need to know.

Quick answer: Your 401(k), 403(b), pension, and other ERISA-qualified retirement plans are 100% exempt in bankruptcy - no dollar limit. IRAs (traditional and Roth) are protected up to $1,512,350. Do NOT cash out retirement to pay debts or avoid filing. That converts protected money into unprotected money.

ERISA Plans: 100% Protected

Under federal law (11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(C) and ERISA section 206(d)), employer-sponsored retirement plans are completely exempt from the bankruptcy estate. This includes:

401(k) plans - no dollar limit on exemption

403(b) plans (tax-sheltered annuities) - fully exempt

Defined benefit pension plans - fully exempt

Profit-sharing plans - fully exempt

457(b) government plans - fully exempt

Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) - fully exempt for federal employees

This protection applies nationwide regardless of state exemption choices. The trustee cannot touch these accounts.

IRAs: Protected Up to $1,512,350

Traditional IRAs and Roth IRAs are protected under 11 U.S.C. section 522(n) up to a combined limit of $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). This limit applies to:

Traditional IRAs

Roth IRAs

SEP-IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection if employer-sponsored)

SIMPLE IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection)

Rollover amounts from an ERISA plan into an IRA retain their unlimited ERISA protection - they do not count against the $1,512,350 cap.

Missouri-Specific Retirement Protections

Missouri does not allow federal exemptions. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Missouri exempts retirement accounts under RSMo 513.430(1)(e): IRAs (including Roth IRAs), 401(k)s, 403(b)s, and other tax-exempt retirement plans are exempt to the extent reasonably necessary for support. MOSERS (Missouri State Employees Retirement System), PSRS/PEERS (teacher retirement), and LAGERS (local government) benefits are fully exempt.

Important: Missouri uses state only (no federal option) exemptions. If you have a choice between state and federal exemptions, compare the total exemption picture - retirement is just one factor.

The Biggest Mistake: Cashing Out Retirement

Every year, thousands of people cash out retirement accounts to pay debts or avoid bankruptcy. This is almost always a mistake because:

You lose the exemption. Money in a retirement account is protected. Money in a bank account is not (or minimally protected).

You owe income taxes on the entire withdrawal amount.

You pay a 10% early withdrawal penalty if under age 59 1/2.

The debts may be dischargeable anyway. Credit card debt, medical debt, and personal loans are wiped out in bankruptcy - you could have kept the retirement money and discharged the debt.

You cannot replace the money. Annual contribution limits mean it takes years to rebuild what you withdrew.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my 401(k) in Missouri bankruptcy?

Yes. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Your 401(k) is completely protected regardless of balance.

Are IRAs protected?

Yes, up to $1,512,350 combined for all traditional and Roth IRAs. Rollover amounts from ERISA plans keep their unlimited protection.

Can I contribute to retirement during Chapter 13?

Generally yes at your existing level. Increasing contributions during the plan may be challenged by the trustee as reducing disposable income.

What about inherited IRAs?

Inherited IRAs are NOT protected in bankruptcy (Clark v. Rameker, 2014). Only IRAs you funded yourself are exempt. Spousal inherited IRAs may be protected if rolled into your own IRA.

Should I cash out retirement to avoid bankruptcy?

Almost never. You lose the exemption, owe taxes, pay penalties, and the debt might have been dischargeable. Talk to a bankruptcy attorney before touching retirement accounts.

Can creditors garnish retirement accounts?

ERISA plans are generally protected from garnishment. Exceptions: IRS tax levies, divorce QDROs, and certain federal criminal penalties.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment

## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Missouri Bankruptcy Guide›Second Mortgage

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Missouri

If your Missouri home is worth less than your first mortgage, Chapter 13 lien stripping can eliminate your second mortgage entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Lien Stripping?

Lien stripping is a powerful tool available in Chapter 13 bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. section 506(a). It allows you to remove a junior mortgage (second mortgage, HELOC, or other junior lien) from your home when:

Your home's current fair market value is less than the balance on your first mortgage

The second mortgage is therefore wholly unsecured - there is no equity supporting it

You file a motion with the W.D. Mo. and E.D. Mo. court asking to strip the lien

Once the Chapter 13 plan is completed, the second mortgage lien is permanently removed from your property.

How It Works

1

Get Home Appraised

Determine your home's fair market value. It must be less than the first mortgage balance for the second mortgage to qualify for stripping.

2

File Lien Strip Motion

Your attorney files a motion to value the collateral and strip the wholly unsecured junior lien. The second mortgage holder can object.

3

Complete the Plan

The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your 3-5 year plan. Upon plan completion and discharge, the lien is permanently removed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Lien stripping is not available in Chapter 7 per Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015). The personal liability on the second mortgage can be discharged, but the lien survives. The creditor cannot collect from you personally, but the lien remains attached to the property.

Chapter 13

Lien stripping is available. If the home value is below the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage lien is stripped and the debt is treated as unsecured. Upon successful plan completion, the lien is permanently removed from your title.

Missouri Homestead Exemption Impact

The Missouri homestead exemption of $15,000 protects your home equity from creditors. In a lien stripping scenario, the exemption is less relevant because the home is underwater - but it becomes critical if property values recover.

Learn more about protecting your home in Missouri →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip a second mortgage in bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes, through a process called lien stripping in Chapter 13. If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, the second mortgage is considered wholly unsecured and can be treated as general unsecured debt in your plan. At completion of the plan, the lien is removed.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 7?

No. The Supreme Court ruled in Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015) that Chapter 7 debtors cannot strip wholly underwater junior liens. Lien stripping is only available in Chapter 13 (and Chapter 11) in Missouri.

What if I have some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home value exceeds the first mortgage balance even slightly, the second mortgage is not wholly unsecured and cannot be stripped. The full second mortgage balance would need to be addressed in your Chapter 13 plan or reaffirmed.

Explore Lien Stripping Options

Learn more about how lien stripping can save your home and reduce your debt.

Lien Stripping Guide
Missouri Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Missouri - Will I Lose My Clearance?
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/security-clearance.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Missouri

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Missouri

Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke your security clearance. In many cases, resolving debt through bankruptcy can actually help your clearance by eliminating financial vulnerability. Here is what clearance holders in Missouri need to know.

Quick answer: Bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. The adjudicative guidelines recognize that taking responsible steps to resolve debt - including filing bankruptcy - can be a mitigating factor. Unresolved, spiraling debt is a bigger clearance risk than filing bankruptcy to resolve it.

Guideline F: Financial Considerations

Security clearance adjudications are governed by the Adjudicative Guidelines, specifically Guideline F (Financial Considerations). The concern is that financial problems can indicate poor self-control, unreliability, or vulnerability to pressure.

However, Guideline F also lists mitigating conditions, including:

The behavior happened under unusual circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and is unlikely to recur

The individual has initiated and is adhering to a good-faith effort to resolve debts - bankruptcy qualifies

The conditions that resulted in the financial problem were largely beyond the person's control

There are clear indications the problem is being resolved or is under control

Filing bankruptcy directly addresses multiple mitigating conditions. It eliminates the debt, demonstrates proactive behavior, and removes the financial vulnerability that is the actual clearance concern.

Missouri Military and Defense Presence

Missouri hosts Fort Leonard Wood, Whiteman Air Force Base, and numerous defense contractors. The St. Louis metro area has a significant defense industry presence (Boeing/defense operations). Kansas City has federal facilities including the NNSA Kansas City National Security Campus. Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance.

Self-Reporting Requirements

If you currently hold a security clearance, you are typically required to self-report significant financial events. This includes:

Filing for bankruptcy (either chapter)

Judgments or liens against you

Significant delinquencies (typically 120+ days past due)

Wage garnishments

Report through your Facility Security Officer (FSO) or Security Manager. Timely self-reporting demonstrates integrity and compliance - two qualities adjudicators value highly.

Failing to report a bankruptcy filing when required is a separate security concern. The cover-up is worse than the event itself. Always report promptly.

Why Bankruptcy Can Help Your Clearance

Without Bankruptcy

Growing debt creates ongoing vulnerability

Debt collectors and creditors pursuing you

Potential garnishments and judgments

Financial stress affects job performance

Adjudicator sees unresolved problems

After Bankruptcy

Debt eliminated or structured into a plan

No more creditor pressure or coercion risk

Clean financial slate going forward

Demonstrates proactive problem-solving

Adjudicator sees responsible resolution

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my security clearance if I file bankruptcy?

No. Bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. It can demonstrate financial responsibility by resolving debt that creates vulnerability.

Should I tell my security officer?

Yes. Self-reporting is typically required and demonstrates integrity. Failure to report is a separate concern.

Can bankruptcy help my clearance?

Yes. Eliminating debt removes financial vulnerability - the actual clearance concern. Adjudicators recognize bankruptcy as a responsible step.

Is Chapter 13 better than Chapter 7 for clearance?

Chapter 13 is sometimes viewed more favorably because it shows a repayment commitment. But Chapter 7 is also recognized as a legitimate debt resolution method.

Can I get a new clearance after bankruptcy?

Yes. Many people receive clearances after bankruptcy. Adjudicators evaluate the whole person, not just the bankruptcy filing.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Springfield, Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/springfield/chapter-13.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Springfield›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Springfield, Missouri

Chapter 13 lets Springfield residents keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years.

Chapter 13 in Springfield

Chapter 13 creates a court-supervised repayment plan. Filed at 222 N. John Q. Hammons Pkwy, Springfield. Particularly useful for saving a home from foreclosure or keeping a car.

Timeline

Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay stops collections.

Day 30: First payment to trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting in Springfield.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments.

Completion: Discharge entered.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a plan?

3-5 years depending on income.

Can I keep my house?

Yes. The automatic stay stops foreclosure. Catch up on payments through your plan.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Springfield, Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/springfield/chapter-7.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Springfield›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Springfield, Missouri

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Springfield residents file in the Springfield Division of W.D. Mo.

Chapter 7 in Springfield

Chapter 7 eliminates credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and other unsecured debts. Filed at 222 N. John Q. Hammons Pkwy, Springfield, MO 65806.

Most Springfield filers keep all property within Missouri exemption limits.

Timeline

Preparation: Documents, credit counseling ($15-25).

Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect.

341 Meeting: ~30 days. Springfield courthouse.

Discharge: 90-120 days after filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 take?

3-4 months from filing to discharge.

What is the means test?

Compares your income to Missouri median. Below median = automatic qualification.

## Missouri Exemptions for Springfield Filers
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/springfield/exemptions.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Springfield›Exemptions

Missouri Exemptions for Springfield Filers

Springfield filers use Missouri state exemptions - the same exemptions that apply across the entire state.

Missouri Exemptions

PropertyAmountStatute

Homestead$15,000RSMo 513.475

Vehicle$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

Household goods$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

Clothing$1,500RSMo 513.430(1)

Wildcard$600 + up to $1,250RSMo 513.430(3)

Tools of trade$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

RetirementUnlimitedRSMo 513.430(10)(e)

Social SecurityUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption?

$15,000 of equity in your primary residence ($30,000 for married couples).

What is the vehicle exemption?

$3,000 per person (RSMo 513.430).

## Springfield Bankruptcy FAQ - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/springfield/faq.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›Springfield›FAQ

Springfield Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to common questions from Springfield residents considering bankruptcy.

Where do I file in Springfield?

Springfield Division of W.D. Mo., 222 N. John Q. Hammons Pkwy, Springfield, MO 65806. Phone: (417) 831-7617.

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400 (most through plan). National cost guide.

What exemptions apply?

Missouri state exemptions. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Wildcard: $600+. Full list.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years.

Will bankruptcy stop garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops garnishment immediately.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do I file?

Springfield Division of W.D. Mo., 222 N. John Q. Hammons Pkwy, Springfield, MO 65806.

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400.

What exemptions apply?

Missouri state exemptions: homestead $15,000, vehicle $3,000, wildcard $600+.

Springfield residents file in the Springfield Division of the Western District of Missouri. Same Missouri exemptions as Kansas City.

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$3,000

Vehicle Exemption

Springfield Courthouse

Western District of Missouri - Springfield Division

Courthouse: 222 N. John Q. Hammons Pkwy, Springfield, MO 65806

Phone: (417) 831-7617 | mow.uscourts.gov

The Springfield Division serves southwest Missouri including Springfield, Joplin, and surrounding counties.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Must pass the means test.

Filing fee: $338

Attorney fees: $1,000-$2,000
Learn more →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay debts over 3-5 years. No means test.

Filing fee: $313

Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000
Learn more →

PropertyExemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + unused homestead

RetirementFully exempt

Full details →

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400.

## The 341 Meeting in St. Louis Bankruptcy - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/341-meeting.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting in St. Louis Bankruptcy

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing about 30 days after filing at the E.D. Mo. courthouse.

St. Louis 341 Meeting Location

E.D. Mo.: 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102

Many meetings are now by phone or video. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most last 5-10 minutes.

What to Bring

Government-issued photo ID

Proof of Social Security number

Most recent tax return

Recent pay stubs

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the 341 meeting in St. Louis?

111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102, or by phone/video.

## The Automatic Stay in St. Louis Bankruptcy - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/automatic-stay.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in St. Louis Bankruptcy

The automatic stay takes effect the instant you file and stops most creditor actions.

What the Stay Stops

Stopped

Wage garnishment

Foreclosure

Repossession

Lawsuits

Creditor calls

Bank levies

Utility shutoffs (20 days)

NOT Stopped

Child support

Criminal proceedings

Tax audits

Post-judgment evictions

Frequently Asked Questions

What does the stay stop?

Wage garnishment, foreclosure, repossession, lawsuits, creditor calls, bank levies, and utility shutoffs.

The Automatic Stay in St. Louis - Your Bankruptcy Shield

The automatic stay is the single most important feature of the Bankruptcy Code for most filers. The moment your petition is filed with the clerk of the Eastern District of Missouri, every creditor in the country is legally barred from taking any collection action against you or your property. The stay is automatic - no judge has to grant it, no hearing has to happen. It just exists the instant your case number is assigned.

The stay is codified in 11 U.S.C. section 362(a). It prohibits creditors from: commencing or continuing lawsuits, enforcing judgments, perfecting or enforcing liens against property of the estate, collecting pre-petition debts, offsetting pre-petition debts against post-petition obligations, and exercising control over property of the estate. For a St. Louis filer facing wage garnishment, pending foreclosure, imminent repossession, utility shutoffs, or harassing collection calls, filing bankruptcy puts an immediate stop to all of it.

Stay violations are taken seriously in the Eastern District of Missouri. If a creditor continues collection activity after receiving notice of the filing, you can file a motion for sanctions under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). The remedy is actual damages (lost wages, emotional distress, attorney fees) plus, in appropriate cases, punitive damages. Creditors who know better but push anyway - typically the same aggressive subprime lenders that generated the underlying debt - end up paying the debtor's attorney fees for the motion plus a punishment amount that can run into five figures.

What the Stay Does Not Cover

The stay has exceptions listed in 11 U.S.C. section 362(b). It does not stop: criminal prosecutions, domestic support collection (child support and alimony), paternity proceedings, tax audits and assessments (but not collection), eviction in some circumstances where the landlord obtained a judgment for possession before filing, and certain regulatory actions by governmental units. If your problem falls into one of these exceptions, the stay will not help - you need a different strategy.

Relief from Stay

Creditors can ask the court to lift the stay in specific cases. The most common motion is a mortgage lender asking to proceed with foreclosure after a Chapter 13 debtor misses plan payments, or an auto lender asking to repossess after a debtor falls behind on a reaffirmed car loan. Relief motions are heard on 14 days' notice and are usually granted if the debtor has no real defense. The best response is to cure the default before the hearing or to propose a workable cure on the record.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does the automatic stay stop a foreclosure sale scheduled for tomorrow in St. Louis?

Yes. The stay is effective the moment your petition is filed with the clerk's office, even if it is 4:59 PM. A foreclosure sale scheduled for the next morning is stopped cold. The lender is legally required to postpone the sale. If the sale proceeds anyway, the sale is void and the lender faces sanctions.

What if I filed bankruptcy once before - does the stay still apply?

It depends. If you had a previous case dismissed within one year of the new filing, the stay terminates automatically 30 days after the new filing unless you file a motion to extend it. If you had two or more cases dismissed in the prior year, no stay arises at all unless you file a motion to impose it within 30 days. This is why rapid refilings after dismissal are risky - the stay you are counting on may not exist.

Can creditors call me after I file bankruptcy in St. Louis?

No. Direct contact with the debtor for collection purposes is a stay violation. Creditors are required to route all communications through your attorney (or, for pro se debtors, through the court). If a creditor continues calling after receiving notice, document every call with time, phone number, and what was said, and raise it with the court as a stay violation.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in St. Louis - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/chapter-13.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. St. Louis residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri.

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13 allows St. Louis residents with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan. You keep your property and repay debts over 3-5 years.

Particularly useful for saving a home from foreclosure, keeping a car, paying tax debts over time, and protecting non-exempt property.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Credit counseling, document gathering.

Day 1: Petition filed at E.D. Mo. Automatic stay takes effect.

Day 30: First plan payment due.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting at 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to trustee.

Completion: Debtor education course. Discharge entered.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

3-5 years. Below-median income may qualify for 3 years; above-median requires 5.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. The automatic stay stops foreclosure immediately. Catch up on missed payments through your plan.

What is the E.D. Mo. dismissal rate?

The Eastern District has significant filing volume. Strong legal representation is critical to completing a Chapter 13 plan.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13
Automatic Stay
341 Meeting
Means Test

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in St. Louis - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/chapter-7.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Here is what St. Louis residents need to know about filing in the Eastern District.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts. A trustee reviews your assets. Most St. Louis Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where filers keep everything within Missouri exemption limits.

Missouri Exemptions in Chapter 7

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000 equity

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Wildcard$600 + up to $1,250 unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Full exemptions →

Chapter 7 Timeline in St. Louis

Preparation: Documents, credit counseling ($15-25).

Filing: Petition filed at E.D. Mo. Automatic stay takes effect.

341 Meeting: ~30 days after filing at 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis.

Debtor education: Second required course ($10-25).

Discharge: 90-120 days after filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, old utility bills, and deficiency balances. Not student loans, recent taxes, or child support.

How long does Chapter 7 take in St. Louis?

3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting at the E.D. Mo. courthouse occurs about 30 days after filing.

What is the Missouri means test threshold?

Single filer: approximately $56,847/year. Increases with household size.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Checklist

St. Louis Bankruptcy Filing Checklist

Everything St. Louis residents need to prepare for bankruptcy filing in the Eastern District.

Documents Needed

Tax returns (last 2 years)

Pay stubs (last 6 months)

Bank statements (last 6 months)

Mortgage/lease statements

Vehicle titles and loans

Credit card statements

Medical bills

Court judgments

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need?

Tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, vehicle titles, mortgage statements, credit card statements, and a list of all debts.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in St. Louis? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/cost.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in St. Louis?

A transparent breakdown of every cost when filing bankruptcy in St. Louis.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling

$1K-$4K

Typical Attorney Fees

Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemChapter 7Chapter 13

Filing fee$338$313

Attorney fees$1,000-$2,000$2,500-$4,000

Credit counseling (2 courses)$25-$50$25-$50

Total estimate$1,400-$2,400$2,900-$4,400

Free and Low-Cost Legal Help

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri - (314) 534-4200. Free representation for qualifying low-income residents.

St. Louis Volunteer Lawyers - pro bono bankruptcy representation.

Fee waiver - Chapter 7 filing fee waived if income below 150% of poverty.

Installment payments - pay filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost?

Filing fee $338, attorney fees $1,000-$2,000, credit counseling $25-50. Total: $1,400-$2,400.

How much does Chapter 13 cost?

Filing fee $313, attorney fees $2,500-$4,000. Most paid through repayment plan.

Is there free legal help in St. Louis?

Legal Services of Eastern Missouri and St. Louis Volunteer Lawyers offer free representation for qualifying individuals.

Check Your Eligibility

## Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions - St. Louis
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/exemptions.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Exemptions

Missouri Bankruptcy Exemptions - St. Louis

St. Louis filers use Missouri state exemptions. Understanding them determines what property you keep in bankruptcy.

Missouri Exemptions - Full List

PropertyAmountStatute

Homestead$15,000RSMo 513.475

Motor vehicle$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

Household goods$3,000 totalRSMo 513.430(1)

Clothing$1,500RSMo 513.430(1)

Wildcard$600 + up to $1,250 unused homesteadRSMo 513.430(3)

Tools of trade$3,000RSMo 513.430(1)

Wedding rings$1,500RSMo 513.430(1)

Health aidsUnlimitedRSMo 513.430(8)

Retirement accountsUnlimitedRSMo 513.430(10)(e)

Social SecurityUnlimited42 U.S.C. 407

Workers' compensationUnlimitedRSMo 287.260

Life insuranceUnlimited (spouse/child)RSMo 513.430(7)

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Missouri homestead exemption?

Up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence ($30,000 for married couples filing jointly).

What is the vehicle exemption?

Missouri protects $3,000 of equity in one motor vehicle (RSMo 513.430).

What is the wildcard?

Missouri wildcard: $600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead exemption. Maximum $1,850.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## St. Louis Bankruptcy FAQ - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/faq.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›FAQ

St. Louis Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the most common questions from St. Louis residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Filing

Where do I file for bankruptcy in St. Louis?

Eastern District of Missouri, 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102. Phone: (314) 244-4500.

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400 (most through plan). Full breakdown.

What is the means test?

Compares your income to Missouri median. Below median = automatic Chapter 7 qualification. Learn more.

Property and Debt

Will I lose my house?

Missouri homestead exemption: $15,000. Chapter 13 lets you keep your home. See exemptions.

Can I keep my car?

Missouri vehicle exemption: $3,000. Chapter 13 lets you keep your car and catch up on payments.

Can medical debt be discharged?

Yes. Medical bills are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Process

How long does it take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 year plan. The 341 meeting is about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops wage garnishment immediately.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit?

Chapter 7: 10 years on credit report. Chapter 13: 7 years. Many filers see improvement within 1-2 years.

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do I file in St. Louis?

Eastern District of Missouri, 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102.

How much does it cost?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400 total. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400 (most through plan).

Can I keep my house?

Missouri homestead exemption: $15,000. In Chapter 13, keep your home and catch up on payments.

How long does it take?

Chapter 7: 3-4 months. Chapter 13: 3-5 years.

Will bankruptcy stop garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay stops wage garnishment immediately upon filing.

Can medical debt be discharged?

Yes. Medical debt is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy in St. Louis, Missouri - Complete Guide
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Bankruptcy in St. Louis, Missouri

St. Louis residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri. With over 120,000 cases since 2008, the E.D. Mo. is one of the busiest bankruptcy courts in the country. This free guide covers everything St. Louis residents need to know.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

120,000+

E.D. Mo. Cases (2008-2024)

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Eliminates most unsecured debts in 3-4 months. Must pass a means test.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

Filing fee: $338

Learn more →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

Filing fee: $313

Learn more →

St. Louis Courthouse

Eastern District of Missouri

Courthouse: 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102

Phone: (314) 244-4500 | moeb.uscourts.gov

All bankruptcy filings for St. Louis City and County residents, as well as most of eastern Missouri, are handled here.

Missouri Exemptions at a Glance

Missouri uses its own exemption system. St. Louis filers use the same Missouri exemptions as all other MO residents:

PropertyMissouri Exemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000 total

Wildcard$600 plus up to $1,250 of unused homestead

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in St. Louis?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7; $2,500-$4,000 for Chapter 13

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Full cost breakdown →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do I file for bankruptcy in St. Louis?

St. Louis residents file in the Eastern District of Missouri, located at 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102.

How much does bankruptcy cost in St. Louis?

Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400 total. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400 (most paid through plan). Full breakdown.

Will I lose my house?

Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity. In Chapter 13, you keep your home while catching up on payments. See exemptions.

See all FAQs →

## Can I Keep My Car in St. Louis Bankruptcy? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/keep-your-car.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Keep Your Car

Can I Keep My Car in St. Louis Bankruptcy?

Most St. Louis residents keep their cars. Missouri protects $3,000 of vehicle equity.

Short answer: In most cases, yes, you can keep your car in bankruptcy.

Missouri Vehicle Exemption: $3,000

Missouri protects up to $3,000 of equity in one motor vehicle (RSMo 513.430). If you owe more than the car is worth, equity is $0 - fully protected. Wildcard adds up to $1,850 more.

Options

Reaffirmation

Keep the car, continue payments.

Redemption

Pay fair market value in lump sum.

Surrender

Return car, deficiency discharged.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car?

In most cases, yes. Missouri protects $3,000 of vehicle equity. In Chapter 13, keep your car regardless.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Can I Keep My House in St. Louis Bankruptcy? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/keep-your-house.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Keep Your House

Can I Keep My House in St. Louis Bankruptcy?

Missouri protects $15,000 of home equity. Chapter 13 can save your home from foreclosure.

In many cases, yes. Chapter 13 is designed to help homeowners save their homes.

Missouri Homestead Exemption: $15,000

Protects up to $15,000 of equity (RSMo 513.475). Married couples: $30,000. Missouri allows non-judicial foreclosure (60-90 days), so timing is critical.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house?

If equity is within $15,000, yes in Chapter 7. Chapter 13 lets you keep your home regardless and catch up on payments.

Check Your Eligibility

## St. Louis Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7? - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/means-test.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Means Test

St. Louis Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines Chapter 7 eligibility. Missouri median income thresholds apply to all St. Louis filers.

2026 Missouri Median Income

HouseholdAnnualMonthly

1$56,847$4,737

2$72,036$6,003

3$85,023$7,085

4$104,670$8,723

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Missouri median income?

Single: $56,847. 2-person: $72,036. 3-person: $85,023. 4-person: $104,670.

How the St. Louis Means Test Qualifies You for Chapter 7

The bankruptcy means test is federal, but the numbers that make or break your case are Missouri-specific. For St. Louis residents filing in the Eastern District of Missouri, the test begins by comparing your annualized six-month household income against the Missouri state median for your household size. Most filers pass here without ever reaching the expense-deduction calculation. Below, we walk through exactly how it works and what to do if you are close to the line.

The six-month lookback is strict: it ends on the last day of the calendar month before you file. If your income dropped sharply in the last month or two, consider whether waiting to file makes the lookback window friendlier. Social Security benefits are excluded from the calculation, and so are VA disability benefits, payments to victims of war crimes, and certain reparations. Everything else - wages, overtime, bonuses, tips, rental income, unemployment, business income, spouse income, and regular contributions from anyone who helps pay your bills - is counted as "current monthly income."

If your annualized CMI is under the Missouri median, you pass automatically. You still fill out Form 122A-1, but the means test is done and you can proceed with the rest of your Chapter 7 schedules. If you are above median, you move to Form 122A-2, where you subtract the IRS National Standards (food, clothing, personal care, out-of-pocket health), the Local Standards specific to St. Louis County and the City of St. Louis (housing, utilities, transportation), plus actual expenses for health insurance, taxes, childcare, court-ordered support, and the secured debt payments coming due in the next 60 months. What is left is your monthly disposable income. If that is below the statutory threshold, you pass.

St. Louis Filing Tips

Document Social Security separately. Trustees review Form 122A-1 Line 14 carefully. Exclude Social Security cleanly so the trustee does not challenge your math.

Use the correct household size. Include everyone who lives in your home and shares expenses, even adult children and unrelated dependents.

Watch the tax refund issue. A tax refund received within the six-month lookback does not count as income - it is a return of your own money - but cash on hand from the refund on filing day is an asset.

Keep your pay stubs. You will need to attach pay advices for the 60-day period before filing under 11 U.S.C. section 521(a)(1)(B)(iv).

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Missouri median income for means test purposes?

The U.S. Trustee updates median income figures every six months or so. Check the current Missouri figures at justice.gov/ust/means-testing before you file. For most filers, the one-person median is in the low-to-mid $50K range and the four-person median is around $95K - but always verify the current number.

Do I really need to pass the means test to file Chapter 13 in St. Louis?

No. Chapter 13 does not have the same means test gate as Chapter 7. Chapter 13 has its own disposable income calculation on Form 122C that determines how much you pay into your plan, but there is no 'qualify or be dismissed' mechanic like Chapter 7.

What if I am self-employed and my income varies month to month?

Self-employed debtors report gross receipts minus ordinary and necessary operating expenses on Form 122A-1 Line 5. You should attach a profit and loss statement for the six-month lookback. Do not net out personal draws - those are how you pay yourself, not a business expense.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in St. Louis - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/medical-debt.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Medical Debt

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Medical debt is fully dischargeable. St. Louis hospitals also offer charity care programs.

Medical Debt Is Dischargeable

Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt fully dischargeable in both chapters. The automatic stay stops all medical debt collection immediately.

St. Louis Hospital Charity Care

Barnes-Jewish Hospital - financial assistance for qualifying patients

SSM Health - charity care and payment plans

Mercy Hospital St. Louis - financial assistance program

BJC HealthCare - system-wide financial assistance

Frequently Asked Questions

Can medical debt be discharged?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable in Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

## Student Loans and Bankruptcy in St. Louis - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/student-loans.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Student Loans

Student Loans and Bankruptcy in St. Louis

Student loans are generally not dischargeable except through an adversary proceeding proving undue hardship.

The Undue Hardship Standard

Most courts use the Brunner test: (1) cannot maintain minimal standard of living, (2) circumstances will persist, (3) good faith repayment efforts. Recent DOJ guidance has made discharge somewhat easier but it remains challenging.

Alternatives

Income-Driven Repayment (IDR)

SAVE Plan

Public Service Loan Forgiveness

Borrower Defense

Frequently Asked Questions

Can student loans be discharged?

Generally no, except through proving undue hardship in a separate adversary proceeding.

Check Your Eligibility

## Bankruptcy in O'Fallon, MO - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/suburbs/ofallon.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›O'Fallon

Bankruptcy in O'Fallon, MO

O'Fallon residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the St. Louis metro area, O'Fallon filers are served by the Eastern District of Missouri.

91,316

Population (est.)

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$3,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

Where to File

Eastern District of Missouri

111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102

Missouri Exemptions

PropertyExemption

Homestead$15,000

Vehicle$3,000

Household goods$3,000

Wildcard$600 + unused homestead

RetirementFully exempt

Bankruptcy Options

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Filing fee: $338. Attorney fees: $1,000-$2,000.
Chapter 7 guide →

Chapter 13

Keep property, repay over 3-5 years. Filing fee: $313. Attorney fees: $2,500-$4,000.
Chapter 13 guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do O'Fallon residents file for bankruptcy?

The Eastern District of Missouri at 111 S. 10th St, Suite 4.625, St. Louis, MO 63102

How much does bankruptcy cost in O'Fallon?

The same as anywhere in Missouri. Chapter 7: $1,400-$2,400. Chapter 13: $2,900-$4,400. Full breakdown.

What exemptions apply?

Missouri state exemptions. Homestead: $15,000. Vehicle: $3,000. Full list.

St. Charles residents considering bankruptcy can find help here. As part of the St. Louis metro area, St. Charles filers are served by the Eastern District of Missouri.

## St. Louis Bankruptcy Timeline - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/timeline.html

HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Timeline

St. Louis Bankruptcy Timeline

A detailed timeline of the bankruptcy process for St. Louis residents filing in the Eastern District.

Chapter 7 Timeline

Preparation: Documents, credit counseling.

Filing: Petition filed at E.D. Mo. Automatic stay takes effect.

341 Meeting: ~30 days. 111 S. 10th St, St. Louis.

Debtor education: Second course required.

Discharge: 90-120 days after filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 take?

3-4 months from filing to discharge.

How long does Chapter 13 take?

3-5 years to complete the repayment plan.

## Stop Wage Garnishment in St. Louis - Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/st-louis/wage-garnishment.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeCh. 7Ch. 13ExemptionsCostFAQKansas CitySt. LouisSpringfieldOBP

Home›St. Louis›Wage Garnishment

Stop Wage Garnishment in St. Louis

Bankruptcy stops wage garnishment immediately. The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed.

Automatic Stay Stops Garnishment

Under 11 U.S.C. section 362, all wage garnishment must stop the moment you file. Your attorney notifies your employer and the creditor.

Missouri garnishment limit: lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or amount exceeding 30x federal minimum wage ($217.50/week).

Frequently Asked Questions

How does bankruptcy stop garnishment?

The automatic stay under section 362 immediately prohibits all wage garnishment for pre-bankruptcy debts.

Stopping Wage Garnishment in St. Louis - How Bankruptcy Works

If a creditor is already garnishing your wages in St. Louis, bankruptcy can stop it the same day you file. The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 is the most powerful injunction in American law - it binds every creditor the moment your petition hits the clerk's docket, even before the judge has reviewed your schedules. For garnishment specifically, the stay halts any further deductions from your paycheck and, in many cases, can recover garnishment payments taken in the 90 days before you filed.

A typical garnishment starts with a lawsuit. The creditor sues, gets a default judgment (most consumer cases are defaults), and then obtains a writ of garnishment served on your employer. Under federal law, creditors can take up to 25 percent of your disposable earnings or the amount by which your weekly earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is less. Missouri law may provide additional protections or slightly different calculations. Child support and tax garnishments have their own rules and can take more.

When you file bankruptcy, your attorney (or you, if pro se) immediately notifies your employer and the garnishing creditor that the stay is in effect. Most employers stop withholding on the next pay cycle. If the employer continues garnishing after notice, they become liable for the improper deduction and you can recover it. The creditor that initiated the garnishment can face sanctions and actual damages for stay violations under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k).

Recovering Pre-Filing Garnishment Payments

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, you may be able to claw back garnishment payments made in the 90 days before filing as "preferential transfers." If the creditor received more than $600 during that window, the trustee can demand the money back, or you can pursue it directly as an exemption avoidance action under section 522(h). This recovery is not automatic - someone has to file the avoidance action - but it is a real tool for recovering thousands of dollars that were garnished before you filed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How fast does garnishment stop after I file bankruptcy in St. Louis?

The automatic stay is effective the moment your petition is filed. In practice, actual withholding stops on the next pay cycle after your employer is notified. Your attorney should fax or email a copy of the filed petition to your employer's payroll department the same day you file.

Can my employer fire me for filing bankruptcy?

No. 11 U.S.C. section 525 prohibits private employers from discharging an employee solely because they filed bankruptcy. Government employers are also prohibited from discriminating in hiring or employment. If your employer retaliates, you have a claim for damages and reinstatement.

Will child support garnishments stop in a St. Louis bankruptcy?

No. 11 U.S.C. section 362(b)(2) exempts domestic support obligations from the automatic stay. Child support and alimony garnishments continue unimpeded through bankruptcy. These debts are also non-dischargeable under section 523(a)(5). If child support arrearage is your problem, Chapter 13 is usually the right tool - it lets you cure the arrearage over the plan term while the current payments continue.

Related resources from the Bankruptcy Transparency Network: means test, Chapter 7 vs. 13, 1328(f) prior-discharge screener, 341 meeting, automatic stay, how to file bankruptcy, student loans, Open Bankruptcy Project.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

## Missouri Bankruptcy Statistics - Filing Data and Trends
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Statistics

Missouri Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024). Missouri ranks above the national average for bankruptcy filings per capita, with particularly high filing rates in the St. Louis and Kansas City metro areas.

2

Federal Districts

$15,000

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Missouri Federal Court Districts

DistrictCases (2008-2024)Primary Location

Western District of Missouri (W.D. Mo.)over 80,000Kansas City

Eastern District of Missouri (E.D. Mo.)over 120,000St. Louis

Key Facts About Bankruptcy in Missouri

Homestead exemption: $15,000 - Missouri's homestead exemption protects up to $15,000 of equity in your primary residence.

Chapter 13 dismissal risk: Nationally, about one-third to two-fifths of Chapter 13 cases end in dismissal. Quality of legal representation is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

Prior filer rate: Across the nation, roughly 25-30% of Chapter 13 filers had a prior bankruptcy case, suggesting many debtors cycle through the system without achieving lasting relief.

Means test: Missouri's median income for a single filer is approximately $50,000/year.

Understanding the Data

Chapter 13 plans run 3-5 years. Cases filed in recent years (2020-2024) are still active if succeeding - only early dismissals have been counted. The most reliable dismissal rates come from mature years (2008-2019) where most cases have reached final outcomes.

Source: Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, 2008-2024. Analysis by Open Bankruptcy Project.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Missouri?

Since 2008, Missouri's districts have processed over 80,000+ bankruptcy cases. Filing volumes peaked around 2010-2011 following the financial crisis and have generally declined since.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate?

Nationally, about 33-40% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Rates vary by district and depend on factors including the local legal market, judicial practices, and the complexity of cases filed. National dismissal rate data.

## What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/stop-paying-bills.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Stop Paying Bills

What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Missouri

Not all debts are created equal. Knowing which bills to stop paying - and which to keep paying - can save you thousands before you file.

Quick answer: Stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged. Keep paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential services (utilities, insurance). Do not make large payments to any single creditor in the 90 days before filing.

Bills You Can Stop Paying

Credit cards: All credit card debt is dischargeable. Stop paying, but stop using the cards too. New charges near filing can be challenged as fraud.

Medical bills: Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable. Collections calls are annoying but temporary.

Personal loans: Unsecured personal loans from banks, online lenders, and finance companies are dischargeable.

Old utility bills: Past-due amounts on closed accounts are dischargeable. (Keep current utilities paid - see below.)

Payday loans: Dischargeable unsecured debt. Payday lenders may threaten criminal action but cannot follow through on threats related to civil debt.

Collection accounts: Debts already in collections are dischargeable. The collection agency loses its right to collect once you file.

Bills You Should Keep Paying

Mortgage (if keeping the house): Falling behind creates arrears that complicate your case. In Chapter 13, you must be current on post-petition payments.

Car loan (if keeping the car): Same logic. Stay current on vehicles you need for work and daily life.

Current utilities: Electricity, water, gas, internet. Utility companies can require a deposit after bankruptcy but cannot shut off service solely because you filed.

Insurance: Auto insurance is legally required in Missouri. Health insurance protects against new medical debt.

Child support/alimony: These are not dischargeable and must be paid. Falling behind creates a priority debt that must be paid in full in Chapter 13.

Taxes: Recent income taxes (within 3 years) are priority debts. Older tax debts may be dischargeable under specific conditions.

The 90-Day Preference Period

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee can claw back payments made to creditors during the 90 days before filing if they exceed $600 to a single creditor. For payments to insiders (family, business partners), the look-back period is one year.

Common mistake: Paying off a credit card or repaying a family loan right before filing. The trustee can sue the recipient to recover that money for the benefit of all creditors. This is called a preferential transfer.

$600+ to one creditor in 90 days - potentially recoverable

Any amount to family in 12 months - potentially recoverable

Regular, ordinary payments (mortgage, car loan, utilities) - generally safe

Frequently Asked Questions

What bills should I stop paying before bankruptcy in Missouri?

Generally, you should continue paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential bills (utilities, insurance). You can typically stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged in bankruptcy. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney before making changes.

Should I stop paying credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Yes, in most cases. Credit card debt is unsecured and will be discharged in bankruptcy. Continuing to pay credit cards before filing wastes money that could go toward filing costs or essential expenses. However, stop making new charges on the cards - using credit cards with no intent to repay is fraud.

What is the preference period in bankruptcy?

The preference period is 90 days before filing (1 year for payments to insiders like family members). Payments of $600 or more to a single creditor during this period can be clawed back by the trustee as preferential transfers. Paying a favorite creditor right before filing can backfire.

Should I keep paying my mortgage before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep your house, yes - keep paying the mortgage. If you plan to surrender the house, you can stop paying. In Chapter 13, mortgage arrears can be cured through the plan, but you must stay current on post-petition payments.

Should I keep paying my car loan before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep the vehicle, continue making payments. If you plan to surrender it, you can stop. In Chapter 13, you can cure arrears through the plan and may be able to reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's value (cramdown) if the loan is more than 910 days old.

Can creditors sue me if I stop paying before filing?

Yes, creditors can file lawsuits and obtain judgments. However, once you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay halts all collection activity. If you plan to file within 30-60 days, the risk of a completed lawsuit is low. A judgment lien, however, can complicate your bankruptcy.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ
