## Surrendering Your Car in Missouri Bankruptcy - Process, Deficiency, Options
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/surrender-car.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Surrendering Your Car in Missouri Bankruptcy

Surrendering Your Car in Missouri Bankruptcy

Surrendering a vehicle in bankruptcy eliminates both the car payment and any deficiency balance. This can be the right choice when you owe more than the car is worth or cannot afford the payments.

Quick answer: Surrender means returning the vehicle to the lender. In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the car sells for) is discharged - you owe nothing. In Chapter 13, the deficiency becomes unsecured debt in your plan. Surrender is often the smartest move for upside-down loans.

How Car Surrender Works

Step-by-Step Process

File bankruptcy and indicate "surrender" on your Statement of Intention (Chapter 7) or plan (Chapter 13)

Automatic stay activates - the lender cannot repossess while the stay is in effect

Return the vehicle - the lender arranges pickup or designates a drop-off location (usually 30-45 days after filing)

Lender sells the vehicle - typically at auction, often for wholesale value

Deficiency balance calculated - what you owe minus sale price

Deficiency discharged - in Chapter 7, the deficiency is eliminated; in Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan

Missouri Vehicle Exemptions

Missouri's motor vehicle exemption ($3,000 per person, RSMo 513.430(1)(5)) is modest. Surrendering an underwater vehicle is common in Missouri. The deficiency balance is discharged in Chapter 7. Missouri's geography - with major metros (KC, STL, Springfield) separated by rural areas - makes personal transportation important outside the metro cores. Kansas City and St. Louis have some public transit, but it is limited.

When Surrender Makes Sense

Consider Surrender When

You owe significantly more than the car is worth (upside down)

The payment is unaffordable

The vehicle has high mileage or needs expensive repairs

You have access to alternative transportation

You can acquire a cheaper replacement vehicle

Interest rate is very high (20%+ on a buy-here-pay-here loan)

Consider Keeping When

You have positive equity (worth more than you owe)

The payment is affordable on your budget

The vehicle is reliable and you need it for work

You are close to paying it off

Chapter 13 cramdown can reduce the balance to current value

Interest rate is reasonable

The Chapter 13 Cramdown Alternative

If your car loan is more than 910 days old (approximately 2.5 years), Chapter 13 offers a powerful alternative to surrender: the cramdown. Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), you can reduce the secured claim to the vehicle's current value and pay that amount through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

Example: You owe $15,000 on a car worth $8,000. If the loan is older than 910 days, you pay $8,000 as a secured claim in your plan. The remaining $7,000 becomes unsecured debt (paid at whatever percentage your plan pays unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar).

The 910-day rule (sometimes called the "hanging paragraph") prevents cramdown on newer vehicle purchases.

Getting a Replacement Vehicle

After surrender, you will need replacement transportation. Options include:

Buy-here-pay-here lots: Available immediately but often have high interest rates and limited selection

Cash purchase: Save up for a reliable used car ($3,000-$5,000 can get a dependable vehicle)

Credit union auto loans: Some credit unions will lend to recent bankruptcy filers, especially members

Subprime auto lenders: Available after discharge, interest rates will be high but can be refinanced later as credit improves

Family or friends: A temporary arrangement while you rebuild

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens when I surrender my car?

You return it, the lender sells it, and the deficiency is discharged in Chapter 7 or becomes unsecured debt in Chapter 13.

Do I have to surrender?

No. You can reaffirm, redeem, or (in Chapter 13) cramdown. Surrender is one option among several.

When do I give up the car?

Typically 30-45 days after filing. The lender arranges pickup or a drop-off. You can drive until then.

Will I owe after surrender?

In Chapter 7, no - the deficiency is discharged. In Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan.

Can I surrender a leased car?

Yes. You can reject the lease in bankruptcy. Early termination fees and remaining lease obligations are dischargeable.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment

## Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Missouri - Trustee Practices, Protecting Your Refund
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/tax-refund-chapter13.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Missouri

Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Missouri

In Chapter 13, the trustee may require you to turn over all or part of your tax refund as additional plan payments. Trustee practices vary by district, and there are strategies to protect some or all of your refund.

Quick answer: Most Chapter 13 trustees require debtors to turn over tax refunds above a threshold amount (typically $1,500-$2,500, but this varies by district). Your plan confirmation order or the trustee's standing instructions will specify requirements. Adjusting W-4 withholding to reduce the refund is one strategy, but must be done carefully to avoid bad-faith arguments.

Why the Trustee Wants Your Tax Refund

In Chapter 13, you commit all projected disposable income to your repayment plan for 3-5 years (11 U.S.C. section 1325(b)). The trustee's position is that a tax refund represents income that was over-withheld during the year - money that should have been available to pay creditors.

The logic: if you receive a $4,000 refund, that is $333/month in excess withholding that was not included in your disposable income calculation. The trustee argues this should go to your creditors.

Missouri Trustee Practices

Missouri has a state income tax (graduated, up to 4.8%). Both federal and Missouri state refunds are at risk. Trustee practices differ significantly between the W.D. Mo. and E.D. Mo. In the W.D. Mo. (Kansas City), the Chapter 13 trustee typically requires turnover of refunds exceeding approximately $2,000. In the E.D. Mo. (St. Louis), practices may differ. Missouri's wildcard exemption ($600, RSMo 513.430(3)) can shelter a small portion of a refund.

Strategies to Protect Your Refund

1. Adjust Your W-4 Withholding

The most effective strategy is to adjust your W-4 so you get more per paycheck and a smaller refund. If your refund is $500, the trustee has little to claim. Do this gradually and early in the plan - a sudden, dramatic change may trigger scrutiny.

2. Plan for the Refund in Your Budget

If you know the trustee will require turnover above $2,000, plan your withholding so your refund falls below that threshold. Use the IRS withholding estimator (irs.gov) to calibrate.

3. Request Permission to Keep the Refund

Some trustees allow debtors to keep refunds for specific purposes: necessary car repairs, medical expenses, or home maintenance. File a motion or request with the trustee explaining the need. Documentation helps (repair estimates, medical bills).

4. Earned Income Credit Arguments

If a significant portion of your refund is the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) or Child Tax Credit, some courts treat these as government benefits rather than disposable income. This argument has mixed results but is worth raising.

What Happens If You Do Not Turn Over the Refund

Failing to turn over a required tax refund can have serious consequences:

Motion to dismiss: The trustee can file a motion to dismiss your Chapter 13 case

Conversion to Chapter 7: The trustee or court may convert your case to Chapter 7

Bad faith finding: Repeated failure can be considered bad faith, affecting your ability to refile

Plan modification: The trustee may seek to modify your plan to increase payments

If you cannot turn over the refund or need to use it for an emergency, file a motion with the court BEFORE spending it. Courts are more sympathetic when you ask permission than when you ask forgiveness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will the trustee take my tax refund?

Most likely some or all of it, depending on the amount and your district's practices. Check your plan terms for the specific threshold.

Can I adjust my withholding?

Yes, within reason. Gradual adjustments to reduce over-withholding are generally acceptable. Dramatic changes may be scrutinized.

What about Earned Income Credit?

Some courts treat EITC differently from regular refund amounts. This is not settled law everywhere - results vary by district.

When must I turn over the refund?

Typically within 30-60 days of filing your return. Check the trustee's standing instructions or your plan confirmation order.

What if I spent the refund already?

Contact your attorney immediately. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss. Being proactive about communicating with the trustee is better than ignoring the requirement.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Missouri
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/timeshare.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Timeshares

Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Missouri

Trapped in a timeshare? Bankruptcy may be the most reliable exit. You can surrender the timeshare and discharge the mortgage, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Quick answer: You can surrender a timeshare in Chapter 7 bankruptcy and discharge all associated debts - mortgage, maintenance fees, assessments, and special levies. This is often faster and cheaper than timeshare exit companies.

Chapter 7: The Clean Exit

Chapter 7 is the most straightforward way to exit a timeshare:

List the timeshare as property on Schedule A/B (real or personal property, depending on how the timeshare is structured)

Indicate surrender on your Statement of Intention (Schedule D/Form 108)

Discharge the debt: The mortgage balance, maintenance fees, assessments, and any other obligations are discharged

Deed transfer: The timeshare company typically handles transferring the deed out of your name after discharge

Important: You are responsible for maintenance fees that accrue between filing and the date the timeshare is actually transferred. Work with the trustee to expedite the surrender.

Chapter 13: Keep or Surrender

Chapter 13 gives you more options:

Surrender through plan: Include the timeshare surrender in your Chapter 13 plan. Pre-petition arrears are treated as unsecured debt, paid at the plan percentage (often 10-30 cents on the dollar).

Keep and pay: If you want to keep the timeshare, continue paying the mortgage and fees. Include any arrears in the plan.

Lien stripping: If the timeshare is worth less than what you owe, you may be able to strip the lien in some circumstances - though this is more common with second mortgages on primary residences.

What Debts Are Covered?

Timeshare mortgage: Discharged in Chapter 7, paid through plan in Chapter 13

Maintenance fees (past due): Discharged as unsecured debt

Special assessments: Discharged as unsecured debt

Exchange program fees: Discharged (RCI, Interval International, etc.)

Collection agency debts: If the timeshare company sold the debt, the buyer's claim is also discharged

Bankruptcy vs. Timeshare Exit Companies

Bankruptcy

Filing fee: $338 (Chapter 7)

Legally binding discharge

Eliminates ALL timeshare debts

Process takes 3-4 months

Court-supervised, transparent

Also eliminates other qualifying debts

Exit Companies

Typical cost: $3,000-$10,000+

No legal guarantee of results

High rate of failure/scams

Process can take 12-24 months

No regulatory oversight

Only addresses the timeshare

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get rid of a timeshare through bankruptcy in Missouri?

Yes. In Chapter 7, you can surrender a timeshare and discharge the remaining obligations - mortgage balance, maintenance fees, and assessments. The timeshare interest is listed as property of the estate and abandoned or surrendered through the bankruptcy process.

Will bankruptcy stop timeshare maintenance fees?

Bankruptcy discharges your personal liability for pre-petition maintenance fees. However, maintenance fees that accrue after filing are your responsibility until the timeshare is actually transferred out of your name. Surrendering in bankruptcy accelerates that process.

What happens to a timeshare mortgage in bankruptcy?

A timeshare mortgage is treated like any other secured debt. In Chapter 7, you can surrender the timeshare and discharge the debt. In Chapter 13, you can either keep paying it or surrender it through the plan. The lender may still foreclose on the timeshare interest itself.

Can I keep my timeshare in Missouri bankruptcy?

Yes. If you want to keep the timeshare, you can reaffirm the debt (Chapter 7) or continue paying through your plan (Chapter 13). You must stay current on mortgage payments, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Are timeshare exit companies legitimate?

Many timeshare exit companies charge thousands of dollars and deliver no results. Bankruptcy is often a more reliable and less expensive way to exit a timeshare. A Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 - far less than most exit company fees.

Does the timeshare HOA get paid in bankruptcy?

Pre-petition HOA/maintenance fees are unsecured debt and typically receive little or nothing in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, they are paid at the same rate as other unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar.

Related Topics

## Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Missouri?
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/travel-chapter-13.html

Missouri Bankruptcy Guide
HomeKansas CitySt. LouisChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Travel in Ch. 13

Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Missouri?

Domestic travel is fine. International travel may require notifying your trustee. The real issue is not where you go - it is what you spend.

Quick answer: Domestic travel during Chapter 13 in Missouri is unrestricted. For international travel, notify your trustee as a precaution. The key rule: do not spend money on luxury travel while your creditors are being paid less than they are owed.

Domestic vs. International Travel

Domestic Travel

No restrictions or permissions needed

No notification to trustee required

Keep spending reasonable

Budget travel is generally fine

Work travel is never an issue

International Travel

No legal prohibition, but notify your trustee

Some trustees want advance notice

Extended trips may raise questions

Keep records of purpose and expenses

Luxury international travel is risky

What Could Cause Problems

Falling behind on plan payments: If you travel and then miss a plan payment, the trustee will connect the dots. This is the fastest way to get your case dismissed.

Lavish spending: Posting photos from expensive resorts while paying creditors 10 cents on the dollar creates a credibility problem. Trustees and creditors can review social media.

Unexplained income: If you are traveling frequently on a budget that says you have no discretionary income, expect questions about undisclosed income or assets.

Extended international trips: Spending months abroad can suggest you have income or assets not disclosed in your schedules.

The safest approach: keep travel modest, stay current on plan payments, and notify your trustee before any international travel.

What Is Always Fine

Work travel: Required by your employer, fully reimbursed - no issue whatsoever

Family emergencies: Travel for funerals, medical emergencies, or family crises

Local/regional day trips: Taking the kids to a state park or visiting family

Budget vacations: A modest road trip or camping trip within your means

Medical travel: Traveling for medical treatment or appointments

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I travel during Chapter 13 in Missouri?

Yes. Domestic travel is generally not restricted during Chapter 13 in Missouri or any other state. International travel may require notifying your trustee, depending on local practice. The key concern is whether travel spending conflicts with your budget and plan obligations.

Do I need court permission to travel during Chapter 13?

For domestic travel, no court permission is needed. For international travel, some trustees and courts want advance notice. There is no statute requiring permission, but your plan requires you to devote all disposable income to creditors - luxury travel could raise issues.

Can the trustee stop me from traveling?

The trustee cannot directly stop you from traveling. However, if you spend money on non-essential travel and then fall behind on plan payments, the trustee can file a motion to dismiss your case. Lavish travel spending can also trigger a motion to modify your plan upward.

Is international travel allowed during Chapter 13?

There is no law prohibiting international travel during Chapter 13. However, best practice is to notify your trustee before international trips. Some trustees view international travel as a sign that you have more disposable income than reported.

What about work travel during Chapter 13?

Business travel is not an issue. If your employer requires travel, that is a legitimate expense. Keep records showing the travel was work-related, especially if questioned by the trustee.

Can I take a vacation during Chapter 13?

Modest vacations are generally acceptable. The concern is extravagant spending while creditors are receiving less than what they are owed. A reasonable road trip or modest vacation is unlikely to cause problems. A cruise or international resort vacation may attract scrutiny.

## Privacy Policy - missouribankruptcy.org
Source: https://missouribankruptcy.org/privacy.html

missouribankruptcy.org

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
